ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Kuih Makmur; mapped to the broader 'cookie (food product)' class as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific entry for Kuih Makmur; A0EJW (Biscuits and cookies) is the closest defensible category-level anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this traditional Malay cookie; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Kuih Makmur is approximately 30 g, equivalent to 2–3 small cookies, as commonly consumed during Hari Raya festive occasions.

nutrient_source.title: Nutrition Analysis of Traditional Festive Cookies
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore festive cookie nutrition analysis. Saturated fat estimated from ghee and peanut fat composition using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (ghee: USDA #04134; peanuts: USDA #16087) as a supplementary reference for fatty acid breakdown. Monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and trans fat values set to -1 as detailed fatty acid fractions are not reported in the HPB source for this specific item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson et al.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kuih Makmur in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 72 (high) based on the closest analogous food: shortbread / butter cookie made with refined white flour and sugar (GI ~70–75 per Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The high refined flour and icing sugar content of Kuih Makmur supports this estimate. Glycemic load per 30 g serving calculated as: (72 × 16.35 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 ≈ 11.8, rounded to 12.5 accounting for sugar contribution.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (120 mg/100 g) and high saturated fat from ghee
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Kuih Makmur to 1–2 cookies per occasion, as the saturated fat from ghee may contribute to arterial stiffness and the sodium content adds to daily sodium load; total daily sodium intake should remain below 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72), high refined carbohydrate (54.5 g/100 g), and high sugar content (22.5 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kuih Makmur is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high GI and significant refined sugar load; if consumed during festive occasions, portion should be strictly limited to one cookie (approximately 10–15 g) and paired with a low-GI food to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat from ghee (~12 g saturated fat/100 g) and high energy density (485 kcal/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high saturated fat content from ghee in Kuih Makmur raises LDL cholesterol risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional festive food only, limiting intake to one small cookie and avoiding regular consumption to protect lipid profiles.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kuih Makmur (also known as Makmur) is a traditional Malay shortbread-style cookie widely enjoyed during Hari Raya Aidilfitri and other Malay festive celebrations in Malaysia and Singapore. The cookie is made from a crumbly, melt-in-the-mouth dough of wheat flour, ghee (clarified butter), and icing sugar, shaped into distinctive leaf or petal forms. The center is filled with a sweet and crunchy mixture of roasted, crushed peanuts and sugar. After baking to a pale golden colour, the cookies are generously dusted with icing sugar, giving them a characteristic snow-white appearance. The texture is delicately crumbly and buttery, dissolving on the tongue to reveal the crunchy peanut filling. Nutritionally, Kuih Makmur is energy-dense at approximately 485 kcal per 100 g, with significant fat (26.5 g, predominantly saturated from ghee), refined carbohydrates (54.5 g), and sugar (22.5 g). The high glycemic index (~72) and sugar content make it unsuitable for regular consumption by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. It is vegetarian and halal. A typical serving is 30 g (2–3 cookies), providing approximately 145 kcal. The cookie is a culturally significant festive treat and should be consumed in moderation outside of celebratory contexts.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Kuih Makmur?
Is Kuih Makmur suitable for diabetics?
What is Makmur cookie made of?
How much sugar is in Kuih Makmur?
What is the glycemic index of Kuih Makmur?
What are traditional Hari Raya cookies?
meal_context: festive snack during Hari Raya Aidilfitri

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values are sourced from HPB Singapore festive cookie nutrition analysis, which is an approved primary regional source. However, the HPB source does not provide a direct per-item breakdown for Kuih Makmur as a standalone entry; values are adapted from the festive cookies category. Saturated fat is estimated from ghee and peanut composition using USDA SR Legacy as a supplementary reference. GI is an analogous estimate based on shortbread/butter cookie data from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, cholesterol, and moisture data for Kuih Makmur are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore festive cookies source or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 2. Saturated fat estimated at 12.0 g/100 g based on ghee (approximately 60% saturated fat) and peanut fat composition from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (ghee: USDA #04134; peanuts: USDA #16087), proportioned to the recipe fat content of 26.5 g/100 g. 3. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate from shortbread/butter cookie data (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care / University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for Kuih Makmur was found in approved sources. 4. Vitamin A value of 150 mcg RAE/100 g is derived from ghee contribution; this is plausible but should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis if available. 5. The dietary tag 'low_sodium' from the input has been removed as 120 mg sodium/100 g does not meet the standard threshold for a low-sodium claim (typically ≤40 mg/100 g per HPB guidelines).
